opp.name: IPG_Spend_June2026
opp.ais_why:
• IPG discussions highlighted 'interest in Zycus's capabilities, particularly in advanced spend analytics and AI-driven decision-making,' with participants wanting to 'replace manual reporting processes with AI-driven solutions.'
• Emphasis was on 'utilizing AI and autonomous agents to uncover savings opportunities,' a direct pull toward agentic capability.
• ANA and Merlin are scoped on a $350K Qualified deal, reinforcing that AI-driven analytics is the buyer's stated objective.

contacts.name: Riz Rusli
contacts.title: Vice President Global Supply Chain Operations

items.quote: We don't have any deadlines, but we're very interested in finding the right tool so we can move forward.

items.area: No quantified business case (savings target / hours / ROI number)
items.quote: everything is kind of a manual pull right now

items.area: Competitive landscape beyond status quo unknown
items.quote: I just pretty much logged in and checked out who has the platform

sources: Avoma discovery call 9 Jun 2026 (Mike Jones, Riz Rusli)
metrics.narrative: The buyer articulated clear qualitative pain on 9 Jun but never quantified it. Mike Jones described their current tool: 'What we have today is basically something that we put together years ago... it doesn't do any, there's no bells and whistles to it.' Riz Rusli framed the cost of the status quo as wasted analyst time: 'we spend a lot of time on a periodical basis to prepare different reports... everything is done manually, and the goal is if we have a platform that can help us pull that report out... then you can utilize your time better by working on more value added projects.' No savings target, no hours-per-week figure, and no ROI number were stated. Richard offered the path to quantify it: a value-engineering study benchmarking IPG spend to produce a board-worthy business case. Quantifying that with named numbers is the move.

champion.narrative: No champion is established. Riz Rusli, Mike Jones, and Dan Novotny are genuinely engaged — they invested three executives in the discovery call and asked detailed, informed questions — but engagement is transactional: there is no advocacy, no introduction to a budget owner, no internal coaching on approval gates, and no competitive defense yet. Mike Jones is the most likely champion candidate (he owns the evaluation, drove the criteria, and pulled IT into the next session), but his sponsorship is so far interest-level, not active selling on Zycus's behalf. The risk is the deal advances on interest alone without an internal coach to navigate the approval chain or surface objections. Developing Mike into a champion by the demo is a priority.

sources: Avoma discovery call 9 Jun 2026 (Mike Jones, Riz Rusli)
competition.narrative: The only named alternative is the status quo: IPG's homegrown, manually-assembled spend-analytics tool that Mike Jones described as having 'no bells and whistles.' No commercial competitor was named by the buyer, no RFP shortlist was disclosed, and Salesforce Competitors__c is empty. However, the buyer was actively shopping at the Gartner Symposium ('I logged in and checked out who has the platform' — Riz), and Mike's statement 'we're very interested in finding the right tool' signals an open search where parallel evaluations are plausible. The current wedge is strong: Zycus's spend-analytics heritage (its first product, built for GE), AI/agentic depth (SpendMiner, autonomous negotiation), and a-la-carte modularity that lets IPG start with analytics and expand. The blind spot is whether other vendors are in the mix; surfacing that at the demo matters.

sources: Avoma discovery call 9 Jun 2026 (Mike Jones, Riz Rusli)
identify_pain.narrative: The pain is clearly articulated and owned by the sourcing/ops leadership. The core problem is a manual, low-capability spend-analytics environment: Mike Jones said their homegrown tool 'gets us some information, but it doesn't do any... there's no bells and whistles,' and 'everything is kind of a manual pull right now.' Riz Rusli reinforced the analyst-time drain of manual periodic reporting for customers and executives, wanting reports 'already ready whenever you need it' so the team can focus on 'value added projects.' The pain was validated externally by a Gartner maturity test that 'highlighted a gap in their existing procurement analytic platform.' Pain owners are Mike Jones (VP Sourcing) and Riz Rusli (VP Supply Chain Ops). The pain is real but not yet quantified.

paper_process.narrative: No contracting mechanics have surfaced. There has been no discussion of MSA/SOW, legal review, procurement process, InfoSec, or contract-expiration forcing functions — expected at 21 days into a Qualified deal. The status-quo is a homegrown internal tool, so there is no incumbent contract to time against. Visibility will come once the demo and value-engineering study establish scope and a price point; surfacing the buyer's procurement and legal path should follow the demo.

economic_buyer.narrative: No economic buyer is identified. The most senior person on the 9 Jun call is Riz Rusli (VP Global Supply Chain Operations); Mike Jones (VP Global Sourcing) is the Salesforce Decision Maker and owns the evaluation. For a $350k SaaS commitment, budget authority likely sits above the two VPs (CFO or SVP Ops level), and budget, approval chain, and exec sponsorship were never discussed. When Richard asked about timeline, Mike said 'we don't have any deadlines, but we're very interested in finding the right tool so we can move forward' — interest, but no funding path. Mapping the budget owner before the demo is a ranked move; the risk is Mike cannot secure sign-off alone, or an EB emerges late and resets pricing.

sources: Avoma discovery call 9 Jun 2026 (Riz Rusli, Mike Jones)
decision_process.narrative: The process is early and informal but visible from the call sequence. The trigger was a Gartner maturity test (~a month before the symposium) that surfaced an analytics gap, followed by the Gartner Symposium where Riz, Dan, and Mike scouted vendors and engaged Zycus at the booth. The buyer's next stated step is a detailed demo, and Mike explicitly widened the room: 'Let's get the IT group in there with us' — signaling IT will join the evaluation. Scheduling runs through a named support person (assistant Aleese Livesey on record). No formal approval chain, procurement channel, or vendor-selection mechanism has been discussed. Reading the participant pattern: three sourcing/ops leaders plus inbound IT, but no finance or C-level yet — the process is still buyer-led discovery, not a structured selection.

sources: Avoma discovery call 9 Jun 2026 (Mike Jones, Dan Novotny, Riz Rusli)
decision_criteria.narrative: The buyer named concrete criteria on 9 Jun, all centered on spend analytics. Mike Jones: a platform that can 'take our data and use it to inform us to drive actions... look at savings opportunities'; category-specific analytics 'to show where suppliers compare to each other within that category and to call out opportunities'; and recurring executive-level dashboards 'that we use for our leadership meetings... a variety of internal meetings and reports' to replace manual pulls. They also probed data requirements: Mike asked 'what's it benchmarking off of' and Dan asked whether full line-level PO data is required 'or do you take what you can get.' Integration with their existing ERP without data silos and a-la-carte modularity were explicit ('Is this an a la carte menu? We can take what we want'). These are confirmed analytics-buying criteria; the formal evaluation criteria (RFP/scoring) are not yet established.

confidence_signals.summary: Engagement is live and positive: three execs on discovery, informed buying questions, IT pulled into next step, and an inbound buyer email reply 29 Jun (1 day ago). No cooling signal.

north_star_verdict.math: Created 9 Jun 2026, 21 days in Qualified as of 30 Jun. Close 30 Dec 2026 is 183 days out, ample for an enterprise cycle. Last buyer touch 29 Jun (1 day ago) confirms live engagement. Early-stage deal moving at expected pace; the only near-term action is converting interest into the scheduled demo.
evidence: Three buyer execs (Riz Rusli VP Global Supply Chain Ops, Mike Jones VP Global Sourcing, Dan Novotny Sr Global Sourcing Mgr) attended the 9 Jun discovery call
Buyer email reply received 29 Jun 2026 (1 day ago) per Salesforce LastActivityDate and incoming EmailMessage
Pipeline forecast is correct for a Qualified deal with no EB, no quantified business case, and no buyer deadline yet
north_star_verdict.headline: Healthy 21-day-old Qualified deal: three buyer execs engaged on 9 Jun discovery, clear analytics pain, buyer reply received 29 Jun; next step is the spend-analytics demo with IT included.

flags: Co-create a quantified value case with Mike Jones and Riz Rusli during or immediately after the IT demo—translate their stated pain ('manual reporting burden, time away from value-added projects') into hours-per-week saved, strategic-capacity unlocked, or cost-of-status-quo, and tie it to the ROI/value-engineering study Richard offered (9 Jun). Make Mike the internal champion by giving him a board-worthy business case he can use to sell Zycus to the Economic Buyer and exec sponsor.

Use the IT-group inclusion at the demo as a multi-threading opportunity—validate technical fit (data ingestion, ERP interoperability, legacy-data normalization) and identify the IT lead who will own integration and InfoSec approval gates. A strong IT champion de-risks the technical-validation gate and accelerates contracting. Also ask 'are you evaluating other spend-analytics platforms alongside us?' to surface the competitive landscape—17 days in with no competition intel is a blind spot.

Build Mike Jones from engaged Decision Maker into active champion—Mike leads the evaluation and has genuine interest but has not yet exhibited champion behaviors (approval-chain coaching, EB introduction, competitive defense, or driving the deal forward on our behalf); during demo and ROI study, demonstrate value that makes Mike a hero internally (quantified time savings, board-worthy business case he can present to CFO), ask coaching questions ('what concerns will the CFO have?', 'who else should be in the room?', 'what will make you successful internally?'), and co-create a mutual close plan that makes Mike the driver and gives him ownership of the win.

Convert Mike Jones into a champion: the upcoming demo is the moment to give him a quantified, board-ready story he can carry to whoever funds a $350k tool, since budget authority almost certainly sits above his VP level.

Confirm the competitive set on the next call: IPG sourced this at the Gartner Symposium where rivals exhibit and named no competitor, so ask directly what else they are evaluating before assuming a status-quo-only race.
best_practice_check.summary: Early-stage deal with strong analytics fit and engaged VPs; the highest-leverage levers are converting interest into the scheduled demo, mapping budget authority above the two VPs, and quantifying the value case.

competitive_position.summary: No named competitor anywhere as of 9 Jun 2026: SF Competitors__c is empty and the buyer named no rival. The live alternative is the status quo, IPG's homegrown manual spend-analytics tool, so this is a status-quo / do-nothing displacement, not a vendor bake-off. A quiet parallel evaluation cannot be ruled out because IPG sourced this at the Gartner Symposium where rival vendors exhibit; confirming the competitive set is an open action, not a known threat.

items.quote: Riz: 'What's the next step, Richard? Is it demo is the next one if we're interested in spend analytics portion of it?' Richard: 'Invite a solution consultant who can walk you through the platform, really get into the technology... half hour could work but if you have an hour I think it would be better.' Mike: 'Let's get the IT group in there with us.' Riz: 'We'll do the same route to work with scheduling and find that an hour spot where we can do a demo.'

items.quote: Richard: 'We could do two things. deltas.subject: Map Economic Buyer and budget approval chain during detailed demo—for a $350k SaaS investment, budget authority likely sits above Mike Jones (VP Sourcing, Decision Maker role) at CFO or SVP Ops level; ask Mike directly 'who typically approves a platform investment of this size at IPG, and when should we engage them?' to avoid late-cycle EB emergence that resets pricing/evaluation/timeline.

deltas.to: Surface competitive landscape immediately and position Zycus's AI differentiation wedge—buyer visited Zycus booth at Gartner Symposium (~5 min, superficial) and
deltas.subject: Surface competitive landscape immediately and position Zycus's AI differentiation wedge—buyer visited Zycus booth at Gartner Symposium (~5 min, superficial) and likely visited other vendors but has disclosed zero competitive intel 17 days in; during demo ask 'are you evaluating other spend analytics platforms?' and 'who else did you meet at Gartner?', then lead with SpendMiner proactive AI agents, autonomous negotiation for tail spend, and 25-year AI innovation heritage as the wedge before a competitor defines the buyer's narrative.

deltas.subject: Build Mike Jones from engaged Decision Maker into active champion—Mike leads the evaluation and has genuine interest but has not yet exhibited champion behaviors (approval-chain coaching, EB introduction, competitive defense, or driving the deal forward on our behalf); during demo and ROI study, demonstrate value that makes Mike a hero internally (quantified time savings, board-worthy business case he can present to CFO), ask coaching questions ('what concerns will the CFO have?', 'who else should be in the room?', 'what will make you successful internally?'), and co-create a mutual close plan that makes Mike the driver and gives him ownership of the win.

deltas.subject: No quantified business case yet—buyer articulated qualitative pain (manual reporting burden, analytics gap per Gartner maturity test) but no savings target, no hours-per-week metric, no ROI number stated on 9 Jun call; risk is buyer interest does not translate to budget allocation without a board-worthy, quantified value case the EB can approve.

deltas.subject: No buyer timeline or forcing function—when Richard asked 'do you have deadlines or timelines?', Mike said 'we don't have any deadlines but we're very interested' (9 Jun); close date 30 Dec 2026 is 187 days out, likely a placeholder; risk is deal drifts without urgency or gets paused if a higher-priority initiative emerges.

deltas.to: Map the Economic Buyer and approval chain at the IT demo (26 Jun or next available)—ask Mike 'once we align on fit and pricing, who approves the budget for a pl
deltas.subject: Map the Economic Buyer and approval chain at the IT demo (26 Jun or next available)—ask Mike 'once we align on fit and pricing, who approves the budget for a platform like this, and what does that approval process look like?' A $350k SaaS deal requires C-level or SVP sign-off; EB is likely above Mike's level (CFO or SVP Ops). Without EB mapped before commercials we risk pricing in a vacuum or Mike lacking authority to advance.

deltas.to: Co-create a quantified value case with Mike Jones and Riz Rusli during or immediately after the IT demo—translate their stated pain ('manual reporting burden, t
deltas.subject: Co-create a quantified value case with Mike Jones and Riz Rusli during or immediately after the IT demo—translate their stated pain ('manual reporting burden, time away from value-added projects') into hours-per-week saved, strategic-capacity unlocked, or cost-of-status-quo, and tie it to the ROI/value-engineering study Richard offered (9 Jun). Make Mike the internal champion by giving him a board-worthy business case he can use to sell Zycus to the Economic Buyer and exec sponsor.

deltas.to: Use the IT-group inclusion at the demo as a multi-threading opportunity—validate technical fit (data ingestion, ERP interoperability, legacy-data normalization)
deltas.subject: Use the IT-group inclusion at the demo as a multi-threading opportunity—validate technical fit (data ingestion, ERP interoperability, legacy-data normalization) and identify the IT lead who will own integration and InfoSec approval gates. A strong IT champion de-risks the technical-validation gate and accelerates contracting. Also ask 'are you evaluating other spend-analytics platforms alongside us?' to surface the competitive landscape—17 days in with no competition intel is a blind spot.

value.risk: Transactional engagement so far—no champion behaviors (no EB introduction, no approval-chain coaching, no competitive intel shared). May not have budget authority for $350k investment (EB likely CFO or SVP Ops level above Mike). Risk: Mike champions internally but cannot secure final sign-off without EB engagement, or deal stalls if Mike loses priority/sponsorship.
value.title: VP, Global Sourcing

value.sentiment: Positive—genuine interest, leads evaluation, requested demo with IT group, asked detailed capability questions (a-la-carte modularity, contract metadata ingestion, autonomous negotiation examples). Stated 'we're very interested in finding the right tool so we can move forward' but 'no deadlines'.

value.sentiment: Engaged and interested; stated 'very interested in finding the right tool' and drove discovery call agenda with specific use-case questions (category analytics, dashboards, savings opportunities); no objections raised.

value.title: VP Global Supply Chain Operations
value.sentiment: Positive—initiated vendor search at Gartner Symposium after Gartner maturity assessment flagged analytics gap, articulated pain (manual reporting burden, time sink preparing executive/customer reports), drove next-step commitment ('we'll work with scheduling to find an hour spot for demo'). Engaged and vocal on value of automating tactical work to focus on strategic projects.

value.quote: Riz: 'What's the next step, Richard? Is it demo is the next one if we're interested in spend analytics portion of it?' Richard: 'Invite a solution consultant who can walk you through the platform, really get into the technology... half hour could work but if you have an hour I think it would be better.' Mike: 'Let's get the IT group in there with us.' Riz: 'We'll do the same route to work with scheduling and find that an hour spot where we can do a demo.'
